$comment: Canonical taxonomy for genres + endingType. Single source of truth read by coop-hunter and fact-checker skills. Hard rule: skills must consult this file before assigning any tag; if a candidate does not match a decision-tree path, log 'taxonomy_ambiguous' and skip — DO NOT invent. UI also reads endingType subtitles from here (see app.js).

tier.note: Always the first element of the genres array. Auto-prepended.

perspective.note: Every game has exactly one primary perspective. If perspective is switchable (e.g. some RPGs), pick the one used most often during co-op gameplay.

mechanic.note: What the player primarily does. Multiple allowed but be conservative — only tag if the mechanic is a defining feature. EXEMPTION: survival-craft games whose defining axis is Survival (structure) may carry NO mechanic tag — Survival anchors the genre on its own (e.g. Valheim, Terraria, Satisfactory). Do not force-fit a wrong mechanic like Action onto a building/automation survival game. If a survival game has an obvious combat focus you MAY add Action, but it is optional.

setting.note: Story/world flavor. Can have 0, 1, or 2; rarely more.

structure.note: Macro-structure of the experience. Can stack (e.g. Open World + Survival).

tier.$rule: Judge the GAME's budget / production scale / ambition — NOT just who published it. Publisher is a strong signal, not the decision: a mid-tier publisher can ship a AAA-scale game, and a major publisher can ship a small one. When publisher and evident scale disagree, SCALE WINS. (This replaced the old 'single-element decision based on publishers[]' rule, which mis-tagged big-budget games like Dead Island 2 and Saints Row: The Third as AA just because Deep Silver published them.)

AAA.definition: Big-budget blockbuster: large team, high production values, heavy marketing. Usually a major publisher (EA, Ubisoft, Sony, MS/Xbox, Take-Two, Square Enix, Capcom, Sega, Bethesda, Bandai Namco, Larian post-2020, Warner Bros) — but classify by the game's evident scale. Dead Island 2 and Saints Row: The Third are AAA despite a mid-tier publisher (Deep Silver/Plaion).

AA.definition: Mid-budget: bigger than a hobbyist indie, smaller than a blockbuster (~20-100 devs). Examples: Frozenbyte, Fatshark, Saber, Supermassive, Arrowhead, Techland, People Can Fly, Coffee Stain, Focus, Annapurna, tinyBuild. Mid-scope games from large studios also land here — e.g. Grounded (Obsidian survival co-op, Xbox-published, but not a blockbuster).

Indie.definition: Small / self-published; studio < ~20. Default when neither AAA scale nor AA mid-budget applies.

First-person.definition: Camera is the player character's eyes. Hands/weapons visible, no body.
decision_tree: Camera anchored at character eye level? → yes
No visible body, only hands/weapons? → yes

Third-person.definition: Camera follows the player character from behind or over-the-shoulder. Full body visible.
decision_tree: Camera follows character from behind/shoulder? → yes
Full player body visible? → yes

Isometric.definition: Any fixed camera looking down at the play area — covers both 3/4 isometric/dimetric views (Hades, The Ascent) and strict bird's-eye top-down views (Monaco, Hotline-style twin-stick). Merged tag: we do not split isometric vs top-down because the boundary is fuzzy and the filter intent is the same ('show me games seen from above').
decision_tree: Is the camera fixed ABOVE the play area (3/4 angle through straight-down)? → yes
Player is rendered small, seen from above rather than behind? → yes

Side-view.definition: 2D camera viewing the action from the side. Classic platformer / fighter / beat-em-up layout.
decision_tree: 2D rendering with the camera at the side? → yes
Player runs/jumps horizontally? → typically yes

Shooter.definition: Primary verb is shooting projectiles at enemies. Aiming is a core skill.
examples_negative: portal-2 (shoots portals, not enemies), vermintide-2 (melee primary)

Action.definition: Real-time verb-driven gameplay with reflexes. Combat, dodging, timing.
examples_negative: powerwash-simulator (relaxation), we-were-here-forever (puzzle), castle-crashers (use Brawler), dirt-5 (use Racing)
Action.narrowing_rule: Do NOT apply to pure puzzle games even when they have reflex elements. AND Action is a FALLBACK: if a more specific mechanic fits (Brawler, Shooter, Platformer, Soulslike, Racing), use THAT and DROP the generic Action unless the game genuinely has a separate distinct real-time loop too. Action had become a catch-all (>50% of the catalog) — reserve it for real-time combat games that no narrower tag describes.

Puzzle.definition: Primary loop is solving puzzles. Logic, spatial, communication, or pattern-based.

Platformer.definition: Core challenge is precise movement: jumping, running, climbing.

RPG.definition: Character progression with stats, abilities, equipment. Build/spec choices matter.

Tactics.definition: Strategic decision-making with movement on a grid or with pause-and-plan.

Stealth.definition: Avoiding detection is a core mechanic with dedicated systems (vision cones, noise meters).

Soulslike.definition: Punishing combat with stamina, deliberate timing, run-back-on-death loop. Lineage from Dark Souls.

Loot.definition: Borderlands-style colored-rarity equipment as a primary reward loop.
examples_negative: dying-light (has loot but not the primary loop)

Adventure.definition: Narrative-led exploration with dialogue, story choices, and discovery as primary mechanics. NARROW JANR — point-and-click lineage. Not 'any game with a story'.
Adventure.narrowing_rule: Apply ONLY if the game is BUILT AROUND narrative and exploration as the verb. If story is incidental (e.g. shooter campaign), DO NOT tag Adventure. After this rule is applied, expect ~10-15 entries to keep the tag (down from the historical ~120).

Brawler.definition: Side-scrolling beat-em-up: advance through stages punching/kicking waves of enemies. Lineage from Final Fight / Streets of Rage. Almost always Side-view perspective.
examples_negative: cuphead (run-and-gun boss-rush), gears-of-war (shooter), guacamelee (metroidvania platformer — though it brawls, Platformer is its core)

Racing.definition: Driving/racing is the core verb — race vehicles toward a finish line, championship, or fastest time.
examples_negative: fling-to-the-finish (physics co-op platformer, not a race), team-sonic-racing (PvP-primary, not in catalog)

Horror.definition: Game uses fear / dread / supernatural threat as a core flavor.

Military.definition: Real-world military setting (WWII, modern combat, special forces).

Open World.definition: Large connected map with non-linear progression.

Survival.definition: Resource management + base building + threats. Hunger/thirst/shelter loop common.

story.filter_subtitle: Narrative campaign with cutscenes and a defined finale.
decision_tree: Has named characters with arcs? → yes
Has cutscenes and a credits/finale beat? → yes
Is the game built around the story (not minor flavor)? → yes

levels.filter_subtitle: Discrete level / mission set with a defined last one. No procedural generation.
decision_tree: Discrete levels or missions in a fixed set? → yes
Last level / mission is reachable as a win-state? → yes
Is the level set NOT procedurally generated? → yes
Story can be weak or absent — what matters is the level structure.
levels.narrowing_rule: A 'last level' must be the TERMINUS OF A SEQUENCE you advance through, not merely the final item in a selectable roster. Cuphead (island -> island -> final boss -> credits) qualifies. A flat, independently-selectable, REPLAYABLE pool of maps/rounds played for score/survival/time/leaderboard does NOT, even if the count is finite (e.g. 5 maps): that is the Phasmophobia / Counter-Strike / DEVOUR pattern = no finish, do NOT classify as 'levels' and do NOT add.

arcade-goal.filter_subtitle: A single defined end-goal: reach the summit, complete the route, escape, defuse. Not a level-set, not a narrative campaign.
decision_tree: Is there ONE clear finish line — a summit / escape / completed route / final defuse — after which the main co-op goal is done? → yes
Is it NOT a numbered set of discrete levels/missions? (that is 'levels') → yes
Is it NOT a narrative campaign with character arcs and cutscenes? (that is 'story') → yes

Length is IRRELEVANT. A Goal run can be 30 minutes or several hours. The old '5-60 min single session, restart for same goal' threshold was WRONG (every Goal game in the catalog already runs 2-12h) and is removed.

roguelite.filter_subtitle: Procedural runs with persistent meta-progress between attempts. Final boss = real ending.
decision_tree: Run is procedurally generated? → yes
There is PERSISTENT progression between runs (unlocks, stats, items)? → yes
Reaching the final boss = recognizable 'beat the game' moment? → yes

survival-goal.filter_subtitle: Open-ended survival with an explicit named win-condition (final boss / structure / rocket).
decision_tree: Game is built around survival mechanics (resources/base/threats)? → yes
There is an EXPLICIT NAMED win-condition or final boss? → yes (Moon Lord, Yagluth, Project Assembly, etc.)
Without the named win-condition: NOT survival-goal (those are endless and forbidden by CLAUDE.md §2).

finish_strength.$note: INTERNAL classifier for the ADD decision and the 🟠 verdict marker. NOT a data field, NOT a tag, and the reasoning labels are NEVER shown on the site. 🟠 is a CAVEAT marker with two independent triggers: a soft finish (fuzzy ending) OR a partial co-op (P2 episodic AND secondary). What ships: the entry (optionally a leading 🟠 + short reason in the verdict) or nothing.
co_op_finish_test: 1. 2-player co-op must EXIST (online or Remote Play Together). None → not a co-op game → do not add (a plain 'not a fit', not a 🟠).

2. Asymmetry and helpers are FINE if P2 is present THROUGHOUT: different-but-constant roles (one drives, one shoots) and a permanent secondary companion always at your side (Tails alongside Sonic; Timespinner's familiar) = full co-op, NO marker.

3. 🟠 ONLY when P2 is BOTH episodic AND secondary: joins only part of the game (e.g. battles only) while one player owns story / world / exploration — the classic JRPG shape (Tales series, Ys, Legend of Mana). Still QUALIFIES (playable + finishable together) → ADD with a leading 🟠 saying co-op is battles-only / P2 is a helper. NOT a reject.

4. Count FINITE CO-OP HOURS — only the co-op content that leads to a finish, not the whole game (Forza Horizon ≈ co-op progression to its milestone, not 80-120h; Drive Beyond Horizons ≈ its ~1-2h Scenario route, not the endless part).
5. The co-op finish content must be > 1 hour, else do not add.
6. Reject outright ONLY: endless contracts/quotas, repeatable-match grinds, or no 2-player co-op at all.
finish_strength.hard: Full co-op + a real finish EVENT (summit, last boss, final mission/route, credits) → ADD, no marker. (PEAK = reach the top.)
finish_strength.soft: ADD with a leading 🟠 + reason, for EITHER trigger: (B) finish is fuzzy — an accumulated status / checklist / score-threshold inside the same loop, not a discrete event (Forza Horizon 5 Hall of Fame; Soulmask Central Core behind a survival grind); OR (A) co-op is partial — P2 episodic AND secondary (Tales/Ys-style battles-only co-op).
finish_strength.none: No finish at all (endless contracts/quotas, repeatable matches, no completed run) OR no 2-player co-op → do NOT add. This is the CLAUDE.md §2 endless ban.

none.filter_subtitle: Each player needs their own copy. Standard online multiplayer.

remote-play.filter_subtitle: Local co-op only, played remotely via Steam Remote Play Together. One copy required.

friend-pass.filter_subtitle: Friend Pass / asymmetric design — one owner can invite a non-owner.
